petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Brazilian law enforcement training officer who works for a Brazilian law enforcement agency and is also an internationally competitive Brazilian jiu-jitsu (BJJ) athlete. He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in education under INA section 203(b)(1)(A), intending to continue developing and providing law enforcement training programs in the United States. He developed a training course that has been included in his agency's national portfolio of training programs since 2018, and he won a medal at the World Master IBJJF Jiu-Jitsu Championship. The matter came before the AAO on a combined motion to reopen and reconsider after the AAO had previously dismissed his appeal, finding that while he met at least three initial evidentiary criteria, he had not shown sustained national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his medal from the World Master International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation (IBJJF) Jiu-Jitsu Championship, competing as a lower-ranked belt in the featherweight class within an age-restricted division (ages 41-45). He also cited instances of U.S. law enforcement agencies training their officers in BJJ and a job offer from a BJJ school to help develop a training program for police officers. He argued the medal constituted a major internationally recognized award and one-time achievement under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5, and that it was related to his field of law enforcement training rather than competitive BJJ. Documentation included IBJJF competition results and a few social media posts from the BJJ school.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted it had already found the petitioner met at least three of the ten initial evidentiary criteria and declined to readjudicate whether the medal constituted a one-time achievement, citing INS v. Bagamasbad. In the final merits context, the AAO found that the petitioner's BJJ knowledge being relevant to his work does not establish that his competition medal constitutes acclaim in the field of law enforcement training, and acclaim as a competitive athlete does not indicate acclaim for teaching the skill (citing USCIS Policy Manual stating competitive athletics and coaching rely on different skill sets). The AAO observed most of his instruction hours were spent teaching firearms courses and his course was only partially about grappling or martial arts. Even if considered in his field, the medal did not rise to national or international acclaim because: (1) he competed at a lower belt ranking (the lowest of eight adult belt ranks), which presumably excluded the highest-skilled athletes; (2) he competed in the featherweight class rather than the open class which is open to all weights and worth more ranking points; (3) the World Master Championship is restricted to athletes at least 30 years old and divided by age (his division was 41-45), unlike the World Jiu-Jitsu Championship which has no such restrictions. The AAO cited the USCIS Policy Manual that limitations on who may compete may indicate a prize is not nationally or internationally significant. The medal was also not acknowledged beyond the IBJJF results website and a few social media posts from the BJJ school. The job offer cited as evidence of acclaim was from one of over a thousand local BJJ schools, not the national/international organization.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence regarding a training course he developed, which has been included in his agency's national portfolio of training programs since 2018 alongside over thirty other courses. He claimed the course was presented to the President of Brazil in 2020, evidenced by a photograph of the President holding the course materials. On motion, he also submitted requests from various Brazilian law enforcement organizations to have their personnel attend the course and a 2022 list of his agency's national courses that includes his course. He provided internal documents and letters of support from his superiors in the agency.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that none of the documentation regarding the training course at the time of the prior decision mentioned the petitioner by name except for internal documents and letters of support from his superiors. The statutory requirement is not influence or success but sustained national or international acclaim. While the record indicates the course has been useful and successful for the agency, it does not establish that the petitioner himself received sustained national or international acclaim for it. Regarding the new evidence on motion, none of the letters requesting participation in the course were addressed to him or mentioned his name. The inclusion of the course in the 2022 course list indicates continued use by the agency but does not establish that acclaim for the petitioner himself rose to a national or international level.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted documentation indicating that in 2018, he was the highest-paid instructor in his unit and the second-highest-paid individual in the overall agency. He received his extra pay for his work in training and curricular development.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO had previously stated the petitioner did not provide sufficient information to establish how his high wages compare with those of other high-paid individuals in his field or for what activities an officer would receive extra pay. While the new citations showed he was highly paid within his agency and received extra pay for training/curricular development work, this evidence establishes success within his agency but does not establish his acclaim beyond his agency, which is necessary to show national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his participation in various international law enforcement conferences, along with a letter from his superior stating that being invited to participate in these events was prestigious and a sign of his importance to the agency. He also cited evidence that his highest responsibilities at the agency centered on developing curricula and training policies, including a letter to Brazil's Federal Congress (May 2022) from a Congressman in Brazil's House of Representatives requesting approval of an award of recognition for the petitioner, addressed to the President of Brazil.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that while the evidence indicates the petitioner's success within his agency, it does not establish his acclaim beyond his agency, which is necessary to show that his acclaim rose to a national or international level. Regarding the May 2022 letter requesting an award from the Federal Congress, there was no indication that this request was approved or that the petitioner was actually given this award. Furthermore, while evidence arising after filing may demonstrate sustained acclaim through adjudication, petitioners must establish eligibility as of the time of filing under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1).

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed both the motion to reconsider and the motion to reopen. For the motion to reconsider, the AAO found the petitioner had not established that the prior decision to dismiss his appeal was incorrect based on the evidence in the record at the time. The sole issue on motion was whether the overall record showed sustained national or international acclaim and that the petitioner was among the small percentage at the very top of his field. The AAO concluded the BJJ medal did not constitute acclaim in his field of law enforcement training, and even if it did, restrictions on belt rank, weight class, and age meant it did not rise to national/international acclaim. His training course was successful for his agency but documentation did not mention him by name outside internal documents. His high salary and conference participation showed success within his agency but not acclaim beyond it. For the motion to reopen, the new evidence (requests to participate in his course, the 2022 course list, and the congressional award request) did not establish that his achievements translated into a level of recognition constituting sustained national or international acclaim or a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress, nor that he was one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
6 USCIS Policy Manual F.2, Appendix: Extraordinary Ability Petitions - First Step of Reviewing Evidence
